evaluations.short_rationale: Both offer highly plausible manual concierge MVPs. Candidate B provides a slightly clearer operational breakdown in its prototype scope, making it easier to immediately execute.
Candidate A relies on feasible live video sessions, whereas Candidate B introduces undefined 'standardized remote interface pads', making its MVP significantly less testable and plausible.
Candidate B proposes a much more realistic starting point by using CSV exports instead of complex automated API connections, making the MVP clearer and more approachable.
Candidate B replaces a complex, difficult-to-build multi-platform ad exclusion sync protocol with a highly testable manual monthly reporting service, vastly improving viability.
Candidate A provides a more concretely defined prototype scope, detailing the exact number of users to test with, whereas B is slightly more vague on implementation.
Candidate B opts for manual user crowd-sourcing rather than complex automated acoustic sentiment mapping, providing a much more bounded and testable initial hypothesis.
Candidate B strips away the 'gamified tagging' in favor of a straightforward curated directory, which is a faster and more effective way to validate core demand.
Candidate A suggests a 'massively parallel' environment, which is highly complex to build. Candidate B correctly scopes this down to a single-player text adventure, vastly improving feasibility.
Candidate A focuses on bounded categorization of two sources, whereas B relies on a vague, complex 'aggregation engine' detecting semantic trends across messy, disparate reviews.
Candidate A keeps the prototype scope entirely bounded to internal user arrangement, avoiding Candidate B's added complexity of dynamically populating external historical anchor points.
Candidate A maps out an explicit, highly testable 10-person pilot structure. Candidate B is missing the core concept text entirely and has a hazier scope.
Candidate B proposes an easily implementable manual pickup service, making for a perfect early-stage test, whereas Candidate A requires building complex climate-controlled automated locker hardware.
Candidate B uses a statically simulated sunlight algorithm, which is much easier and cheaper to prototype than integrating real-time local meteorological APIs as suggested in A.
While B is trivially simple to build, A explicitly addresses the stated user problem (comparing actual performance against recommended/optimal care) making it a much more useful test.
Candidate B proposes turning the solution into a clear manual moderation service with specific testing steps, which is highly actionable for a founder to prototype immediately.
Candidate B brilliantly defines an ultra-low friction MVP (a neighborhood WhatsApp group) to test the core assumption, bypassing the heavy development required for A's custom geolocated map.
Candidate B wisely substitutes complex camera-vision OCR with manual inventory input, preserving the novel RPG core concept while making it significantly more feasible to prototype.
Candidate A provides a much more distinct and well-defined prototype scope (designing two buggy snippets with UI) compared to B's slightly abstract approach.
Candidate A offers a highly actionable, bounded test (sourcing surplus from local markets for 5 lunches), while B relies on harder-to-secure partnerships with high-end food distributors.
Candidate B provides a much clearer, actionable prototype outline (building a timeline for one complex recipe) than A's slightly abstract description.
Candidate A focuses on a feasible, low-fidelity test using standard video calls, avoiding the extremely difficult synchronized smart-stove hardware required by B.
Candidate B offers a clearer, more explicit MVP scope by outlining variables, form construction, and simple logic testing, making it more robust.
Candidate A wisely substitutes complex live hardware API queries with manual reporting via email for the pilot, making the test vastly more plausible and realistic.
Candidate A translates a complex AI-driven concept into a standard, highly feasible branching narrative game mechanic, resulting in a much clearer prototype scope.
Both are nearly identical sensory-deprivation dining concepts, but B provides slightly crisper articulation of the MVP steps and execution plan.
Candidate A focuses on a simple, bounded manual entry web form rather than dealing with the complexities of parsing disparate vendor PDF invoices like B.
Candidate A describes an excellent concierge MVP, using a manual spreadsheet for routing instead of trying to build a complex logistics platform from day one.
Candidate B reduces the complex 'background summary agent' into a simple, user-initiated 'save to ticket' Slack bot, greatly improving technical plausibility and security.

Candidate A provides a simple, coherent physical kit testing structure, whereas B proposes coordinating the timed arrival of physical goods simultaneously, which is an unnecessary logistical nightmare.
Candidate A outlines a bounded scraping script with a defined rubric, while B relies on vague, complex 'autonomous social reading' to rank candidate mindshare.
Candidate B is more straightforward, defining exact testable steps for its audio maze MVP, making it a better, slightly more accessible starting point than A.

Candidate A provides a feasible text-parsing MVP. Candidate B promises highly complex 'shadowing' agents capable of parsing collaborative whiteboards, which introduces excessive technical risk for an MVP.
Both describe an interesting isometric visualization for project management. Candidate B defines the basic interface requirements slightly more clearly for a first-pass prototype.
Candidate B reduces complex, real-time legislative feed monitoring into a highly practical text-comparison engine that highlights keyword overlaps, creating a perfect MVP.
Both propose nearly identical, excellent low-code Google Sheets MVPs. Candidate B spells out the execution loop slightly more clearly in its prototype scope.

Candidate A provides a highly bounded approach (time-stamped clips for specific orders), whereas B proposes unscalable 24-hour live web feeds, which introduces massive privacy and infrastructure risks.
Candidate A suggests a perfect manual MVP—a mobile cart observing traffic—while B jumps straight to designing hardware vending kiosks integrated with dynamic transit APIs.
Candidate B describes a slightly clearer, less ambiguous prototype scope ('simple search/filter' versus A's vaguer 'filtered by proximity thresholds'), making it slightly easier to build.
Candidate A smartly bounds the MVP by partnering with a local community garden, while B relies on accessing municipal processing pipelines, which is administratively difficult for an MVP.
Both offer clear database MVP scopes. Candidate B defines the output slightly better by explicitly listing the inclusion of a simple messaging feature alongside search/filter.
Candidate B outlines the technical steps to build the prototype (networking logic, line/color roles), giving a developer a much clearer implementation path.
Candidate A scopes a highly achievable no-code MVP using interactive PDFs or Notion, making it immediately testable compared to building a custom SaaS dashboard.
Candidate A tests the demand curve using simple potted plants, effectively bypassing the expensive and complex 'harvest substrate cassette' hardware prototyping required in B.
Candidate A defines the exact support mechanism (PDF/video guides) required for users alongside the rental, clarifying the MVP loop much better than B.
Candidate B presents a standard, highly viable MVP of stocking local parts, whereas Candidate A requires inventing and manufacturing a 'modular machine heart chassis', which is far out of scope.
Candidate A tests the behavioral assumption perfectly with a manual dashboard, while B introduces massive technical friction by attempting to route micro-transactions via blockchain smart-contracts.
Candidate A clearly outlines the exact functionality to build (image upload + text annotation hotspots), whereas B's description remains slightly abstract in execution.
Candidate B proposes a simple, manual rank tracking and email alert system, which is far easier to bootstrap than Candidate A's complex mapping of SEO drops directly to specific snippet text edits.
Candidate B breaks down the prototype into clear developmental steps (basic geofencing, one audio file, simple UI map), making it a tighter MVP specification.

Candidate A suggests a feasible rules-based calendar tag, avoiding the high technical risk and ambiguity of Candidate B's 'impact analysis agent' predicting schedule collapse based on historical habits.
Candidate A provides a clearer technical pathway (matching steps to a track's beat) rather than generating complex, procedural audio overlays dynamically based on speed.
Both offer an identical closed Slack group MVP. Candidate A slightly expands on the operational steps (facilitating connections, monitoring quality), making it a more actionable plan.
Candidate B proposes a feasible watcher script that passively alerts users to violations, avoiding A's risky, destructive behavior of actively moving legal files automatically based on metadata.
Candidate B correctly scopes this as a manual matchmaking board concierge test, entirely sidestepping the massive technical challenge of building an AI to verify obscure legacy code skills.
Candidate A overcomplicates the MVP with 'biometric and linguistic sentiment ratings'. Candidate B cleverly scales this back to an achievable iterative snack box test using manual written feedback.
Candidate A provides a feasible bounded script to extract data from known schemas, avoiding B's riskier proposition of an autonomous agent reading from email feeds.

Candidate A grounds the MVP in straightforward food prep techniques, while B uses vague terms like 'distributed subscription for dehydration/freezing labor as an utility', making execution less clear.
Candidate A acts as a safer 'decision support' tool requiring manual approval. B pushes for 'self-modifying rules' updating ERPs automatically, carrying extreme supply-chain risk for an MVP.
Candidate A defines the deliverable perfectly (templates and a 3-video training suite) as a simple digital product test, making execution and scoping obvious compared to B.
Candidate B isolates the core testing mechanism (QR codes for preferences) cleanly, dropping Candidate A's unnecessarily complicated physical 'bin monitoring' hardware integrations.
